title: Biology Unit 2 - Cells, Development, Biodiversity and Conservation

parts.text: Which part of a plant cell stores starch?
parts.answer: A

parts.text: How many of the following statements about starch are correct? 1. it has a compact shape 2. it contains 1,6 glycosidic bonds only 3. it is a polymer of β-glucose 4. it is a polypeptide
parts.answer: A

parts.text: How many of the following contain cellulose? 1. phloem 2. sclerenchyma 3. vacuole 4. xylem
parts.answer: C

parts.text: Microfibrils are composed of cellulose. The photograph shows the arrangement of microfibrils in a plant cell wall, as seen using an electron microscope. Explain how the structures of cellulose and microfibrils increase the strength of a plant cell wall. Use the information in the photograph to support your answer.
parts.answer: Cellulose molecules are straight chains held together by hydrogen bonds to form strong microfibrils; microfibrils are arranged in parallel/layers providing strength; the layered arrangement shown in the photograph creates a strong mesh/matrix; microfibrils provide tensile strength/resistance to stretching

questions.question: All organisms contain one or more cells.

parts.text: In which of the following is a cell membrane present?
parts.answer: D

parts.text: In which of the following is a cell wall present?
parts.answer: C

parts.text: Sailor's eyeball (Valonia ventricosa) is a single-celled, spherical organism. The diagram shows one of these cells. The diameter of this cell is 25 μm. Calculate the magnification of the diagram.
parts.answer: Measure diameter on diagram and divide by 25 μm; e.g. if diameter = 50 mm (50000 μm), magnification = 50000/25 = 2000×

parts.text: This cell contains chloroplasts. State the function of these chloroplasts.
parts.answer: Photosynthesis / production of glucose/organic compounds

parts.text: This cell is not a prokaryotic cell as it contains chloroplasts. Give one other reason why this organism is not a prokaryotic cell.
parts.answer: It has a nucleus / membrane-bound organelles / it is large (25 μm) / has chloroplasts

parts.text: The photograph shows part of two adjoining plant cells, as seen using an electron microscope. Name the part labelled X.
parts.answer: Plasmodesmata

parts.text: Explain the function of the part labelled X.
parts.answer: Channels for transport/communication between adjacent plant cells; allow movement of water/solutes/ions/small molecules; enable cell-to-cell communication/coordination; maintain cytoplasmic continuity

parts.text: The diagrams show cells from a plant. The cells are in different stages of division. These cells do not show the correct order for the process of division by mitosis. Which of the following shows the correct order for the process of division by mitosis?
parts.answer: D

parts.text: The graph shows the changes in the DNA content of a cell during one cell cycle. Explain the changes in the DNA content as shown by this graph.
parts.answer: From 0-6 hours: DNA content constant at 2 a.u. (G1 phase - cell growth/preparation); 6-10 hours: DNA content doubles from 2 to 4 a.u. (S phase - DNA replication/synthesis); 10-15 hours: DNA content constant at 4 a.u. (G2 and mitosis - cell prepares for division); 15 hours: DNA content halves to 2 a.u. (cytokinesis - cell divides into two daughter cells); 15-25 hours: DNA content constant at 2 a.u. (daughter cells in interphase)

parts.text: Complete the graph to show how the DNA content would change if the cell had undergone meiosis to form gametes.
parts.answer: DNA replicates to 4 a.u. (as in mitosis); DNA halves to 2 a.u. after meiosis I; DNA halves to 1 a.u. after meiosis II; final DNA content 1 a.u. in gametes; graph should show steps at appropriate time points

questions.question: Plant-based products can replace plastics produced from oil. Wheat bran comes from the outer layers of grains of wheat.

parts.text: The diagram shows the structure and composition of a grain of wheat. Wheat bran contains 43% fibre. A grain of wheat has a mass of 48 mg. Calculate the mass of fibre in the wheat bran of this grain of wheat. Give your answer to two significant figures.
parts.answer: Mass of bran = 48 mg × 0.14 = 6.72 mg; Mass of fibre = 6.72 mg × 0.43 = 2.8896 mg; Answer to 2 s.f. = 2.9 mg

parts.text: Explain the advantages of using cutlery and plates made from wheat bran instead of oil-based plastics.
parts.answer: Wheat bran is renewable/biodegradable/sustainable; reduces use of fossil fuels/oil; reduces pollution/waste in environment; can be composted/returns nutrients to soil; reduces carbon footprint compared to plastic production

parts.text: The endosperm of a grain of wheat develops from the endosperm nucleus, formed during fertilisation. Explain the role of the pollen tube and nuclei in the formation of the endosperm nucleus.
parts.answer: Pollen tube grows down the style to the ovary/embryo sac; carries two male nuclei; one male nucleus fuses with the egg cell to form zygote; second male nucleus fuses with two polar nuclei to form endosperm nucleus (triple fusion); this is double fertilisation

questions.question: The five stages of cancer are used to describe the size and spread of a cancer in a human body.

parts.text: The mitotic index of a tissue can be used to determine the stage of cancer. A higher mitotic index is usually linked to a later stage of cancer. State what is meant by the term tissue.
parts.answer: A group of similar cells working together to perform a specific function / group of cells with similar structure and function

parts.text: Using the data in the table, determine the stages of cancer in patients P and R. The table shows the number of cells that were counted in each stage of the cell cycle per mm² of tissue.
parts.answer: Patient P mitotic index = (1+3+0+0)/(16+1+3+0+0) × 100 = 20%; Patient R mitotic index = (2+2+3+2)/(11+2+2+3+2) × 100 = 45%; Higher mitotic index indicates later stage; Patient R has later stage than Patient P

parts.text: The graph shows the probability of survival for different stages of a cancer, after diagnosis. Compare and contrast the probabilities of survival for the different stages of this cancer, as shown by the graph.
parts.answer: Compare: All stages show decreasing survival over time; Stage I has highest survival at all time points; Stage IV has lowest survival at all time points; Contrast: Stage I shows slow decline (85% at 14 years); Stage IV shows rapid decline (to ~10% by 3 years); Stage II intermediate between I and IV; gap between stages widens over time

parts.text: Anticancer drugs have to undergo double-blind trials before they are used to treat patients. Describe how a placebo is used in a double-blind trial.
parts.answer: Placebo is an inactive substance/control given to some patients; patients do not know if they receive drug or placebo; doctors/researchers also do not know; used to compare effects of drug against control; eliminates bias/psychological effects

questions.question: Stomach ulcers are sores that develop in the lining of the stomach. The mucus produced by the stomach prevents bacteria from reaching the lining of the stomach. Stomach ulcers can be caused by infection with the bacterium Helicobacter pylori. These bacteria reproduce in the stomach as it provides all the conditions required for the growth of bacteria.

parts.text: Explain the conditions needed for the growth of bacteria.
parts.answer: Suitable temperature (optimum for enzyme activity); moisture/water (for metabolic reactions/chemical processes); nutrients/food source (for energy/growth); suitable pH (not too acidic/alkaline); oxygen (for aerobic bacteria) or anaerobic conditions (for anaerobic bacteria); absence of antibiotics/competition

parts.text: The plant Cibotium barometz is native to several countries in East Asia. The photograph shows hairs on the surface of this plant. Extracts from the hairs on this plant have antimicrobial properties. An investigation compared the use of this extract and the drug omeprazole to treat stomach ulcers in rats. The mass of mucus and the area of ulcer were measured in five groups of rats. The table shows the treatments and the results of this investigation. Calculate the percentage decrease in the mean areas of ulcer of the rats treated with omeprazole compared with the control rats with ulcer.
Explain the results of this investigation. Use all the information in Question 6 to support your answer.
parts.answer: Control rats with ulcer had low mucus (0.75g) and large ulcer area (802.71 mm²) showing H. pylori infection reduces mucus and causes ulcers. Omeprazole treatment reduced ulcer area dramatically to 95.71 mm² (88% decrease) and increased mucus to 1.92g, showing it is effective. Plant extract treatments also reduced ulcer area (202.80 mm² at 125 mg/kg; 172.80 mm² at 500 mg/kg) and increased mucus (1.87g and 2.17g), showing antimicrobial properties. Higher dose of extract (500 mg/kg) was more effective than lower dose (125 mg/kg). Extract was less effective than omeprazole but still beneficial. Control rats with no ulcer had highest mucus (2.28g) and zero ulcer area, showing normal stomach protection.

questions.question: Meiosis increases genetic variation through independent assortment and crossing over.

parts.text: In which stage of meiosis does independent assortment occur?
parts.answer: A

parts.text: In which stage of meiosis does crossing over occur?
parts.answer: C

parts.text: Genetic variation increases due to the production of new combinations of alleles. A cell has gene A and gene B with alleles A, a and B, b. Meiosis could produce the following percentages of gametes containing the combinations of alleles shown in the table. Explain why the percentages are not 25% for each combination of alleles.
parts.answer: The genes are linked/on the same chromosome; AB and ab combinations are parental types (48% each); Ab and aB are recombinant types (2% each); crossing over occurs rarely between these genes; the genes are close together on the chromosome; independent assortment does not apply to linked genes; recombinant frequencies are much lower than 50% expected for independent assortment

parts.text: More than one type of protein can be synthesised from the RNA produced from one gene. The diagram shows parts of this process. Describe how more than one type of protein can be synthesised from the RNA produced from one gene. Use the information in the diagram to support your answer.
parts.answer: Gene contains exons (coding regions P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, W) and introns; transcription produces pre-mRNA containing all exons and introns; splicing removes introns and some exons; different combinations of exons can be retained in mature mRNA (alternative splicing); as shown in diagram, pre-mRNA with P, Q, R, S, T, U, V, W becomes mature mRNA with Q, S, U, W; different mature mRNA produces different polypeptides/proteins; one gene can code for multiple proteins

parts.text: The graphs show the activity of two genes, T and U, as a mouse zygote (fertilised egg cell) develops into a blastocyst. Comment on the changes in the activity of these two genes.
parts.answer: Gene T: activity increases from zygote to 4-cell/8-cell stage then decreases to blastocyst; highest activity at morula stage; Gene U: activity remains low/constant until 8-cell stage then increases rapidly to blastocyst; genes show opposite patterns; Gene T active early in development, Gene U active late; Gene T may be involved in early cleavage divisions, Gene U in blastocyst formation/differentiation

questions.question: The photograph shows a Tristan albatross. The Tristan albatross is endemic to a few small islands in the South Atlantic Ocean. The adult birds nest on these islands and fly long distances in search of food. They feed on fish, octopus and squid. The Tristan albatross is critically endangered, with 4 500 of these birds left in the wild.

parts.text: Describe two anatomical adaptations of the Tristan albatross that enable it to occupy its niche. Use the information in the photograph to support your answer.
parts.answer: Large wingspan for efficient soaring flight over long distances to find food; streamlined body shape reduces air resistance for efficient flight; sharp beak for catching/grasping fish/squid/octopus; webbed feet for swimming/diving to catch prey

parts.text: It has been predicted that the population will continue to decline by 5.3% per year. Calculate the predicted albatross population after one year.

The Tristan albatross (Diomedea dabbenena) was once classified as the same species as the wandering albatross (Diomedea exulans). The photograph shows a wandering albatross. Suggest why the Tristan albatross and wandering albatross were once classified as the same species.
parts.answer: They look similar/identical in appearance; share anatomical features/adaptations; both are large seabirds with similar morphology; can interbreed/produce fertile offspring; similar behaviour/ecological niche

parts.text: Tristan albatross chicks in nests on one island have been found to be at risk of predation from mice. The diagram shows an albatross chick and two mice on this island. Over a long period of time, mice on this island have become 50% larger than normal mice. It has been suggested that the mice from this island are a new species. Suggest how the mice on this island have evolved to become a new species.
parts.answer: Isolation on island separated from mainland mice; genetic variation present in original population; larger mice had selective advantage (could prey on albatross chicks/better survival); natural selection favoured larger mice; over many generations frequency of genes for larger size increased; accumulated genetic differences from mainland mice; became reproductively isolated; speciation occurred

parts.text: Scientists have suggested that the Tristan albatross could be conserved. Some suggestions are conservation strategies on the island where the mice are found. Some suggestions involve zoos in other countries. Explain how the Tristan albatross could be conserved. Use the information in Question 8 to support your answer.
parts.answer: Use in situ conservation on nesting islands: protect breeding sites and prevent disturbance; control/eradicate invasive mice (e.g. trapping/poisoning) to reduce chick predation; monitor nests and protect chicks/eggs. Use ex situ conservation: captive breeding programmes in zoos in other countries to increase numbers; coordinate breeding to maximise genetic diversity (studbook, avoid inbreeding); reintroduce captive-bred birds/young to nesting islands; international cooperation and long-term population monitoring to ensure numbers recover.
